steps.message:
<uploaded_files>/app</uploaded_files>

# SEV-1 INCIDENT - RELEASE BLOCKER
**Title:** Statements are filing some payments under the wrong kind of transaction, or dropping the payee name
**Owner:** Payments Operations
**Disposition:** Do not ship this release until it is resolved

## What customers reported

Over the past few days support has collected a run of tickets that all share one
shape: the money itself moved fine, but the app files the payment under a category
that does not match what the customer actually did, or shows the wrong name - or
no name at all - beside it. In every case the row that misbehaves carries one very
particular, uncommon form of raw statement text; everyday rows are labeled exactly
as before. The tickets below are anonymized, but the raw narration each customer
pasted is reproduced verbatim, because that raw text is what makes each case
reproducible.

> "I wrote a cheque against my account and the app just lists it as a general
> transfer now - nothing marks it as a cheque anymore. My earlier cheques were
> tagged correctly. The only thing sitting in that row's note is the cheque's own
> number, nothing else around it."

> "My salary came in and the amount is right, but where my employer's name should
> be the app now just prints the word `NACH`. The raw text on that row looks like
> `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES` - it used to lift the employer's name off the end
> of that reference, and now it is picking up the wrong piece of it."

> "On some transfers that come in, the sender's name is blank now. Those rows all
> share the same reference layout - a short block of letters, then a long string
> of digits, then the name - for example `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR`. The name
> sitting at the end just is not being pulled through anymore; the name column
> comes out empty."

The same batch of complaints also covers two quieter cases that are about the
category rather than the name. The token confirmation charge a bank posts when a
new auto-debit mandate is set up - a small nominal amount, with the note flagging
it as a mandate - is being filed as an ordinary transfer instead of being
recognized as a verification entry. And a refund that came back on a card is
landing as a plain refund for some customers when it should read as a card
reversal; a customer on a different type of account sees the same kind of refund
labeled correctly, so the wrong label appears to depend on the account category.

## Scope of impact

Everyday activity - normal purchases, routine transfers, regular deposits - is
categorized and named correctly, which is precisely why this slipped through QA and
why our monitoring dashboards looked healthy. The breakage is confined to a small
set of unusual transaction forms - among them a note or reference that is exactly
a certain number of digits wide - each one hinging on some exact shape of the raw
row rather than anything about the customer, and each form individually uncommon. But they occur right across the customer base, so they
generate a steady trickle of "why is this labeled wrong" tickets and, more
damaging, they poison the spending-category summaries and payee histories that
several downstream features rely on. That combination is why we are treating it
as ship-blocking rather than a backlog item.

## What we need from engineering

The labeling path is fully deterministic and runs offline - hand it the same
transaction and it reproduces the same wrong answer every time - so each of these
is reproducible on demand and can be pinned down exactly. Get the affected forms
labeling and naming correctly again, and do it without disturbing any transaction
that is already labeled right today. A fix that rescues these cases but nudges the
behavior of ordinary, currently-correct transactions is not acceptable: the pass
condition is simply that the specific problem payments described above come out
right and everything else stays exactly where it is.

steps.reasoning_content:
Let me look at the git history to understand if there was a recent change that broke these. But there's only one commit. Let me look at the structure and the relevant scripts. The issues:
1. Cheque labeled as general transfer - note contains only cheque number
2. Salary NACH: `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES` - picks up wrong piece (NACH instead of employer name)
3. Transfer sender name blank: `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR` - name not pulled
4. Auto-debit mandate token charge filed as ordinary transfer instead of verification entry
5. Card refund landing as plain refund instead of card reversal - depends on account category

Let me explore the relevant utility scripts.

steps.reasoning_content:
So in SBI, index=1 is used with patterns that have 2 capture groups (SBI_AUTOPAY7, SBI_AUTOPAY8, SBI_AUTOPAY9). Those patterns have 2 groups so index=1 is the second group. Those are likely correct.

The IDBI line 110 uses index=1 with a single capture group pattern `[0-9]{10,} (.*)`. That's the bug -> should be index=0.

Now let me handle the other cases. Let me set up an environment to actually run tests. Let me check setup.py and dependencies.

Actually, let me reconsider the scope of the task. There are 5 cases. I've identified:
- Case 2: IDBI.py line 83 `sep_by_dash(desc,2,3)` -> should be `sep_by_dash(desc,3,4)` for `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES`.
- Case 3: IDBI.py line 110 `index=1` -> `index=0` for `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR`.

Wait, but let me double check case 2. The description is `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES`. Line 14: `^ACH|^ETPC ACH` & CREDIT -> AUTO_CREDIT. So category AUTO_CREDIT. Then partyName line 83 `^ACH-BD-NACH` -> sep_by_dash(desc,2,3) = element at index 2 = "NACH". Indeed wrong; should be index 3 = "ACME INDUSTRIES". 

But wait — there's also line 84 `^ACH-BD-` -> sep_by_dash(desc,2,3) and line 81 `ETPC.*ACH-BD-` -> sep_by_dash(desc,2,3). For a plain `ACH-BD-SOMETHING` (no NACH), split is ["ACH","BD","SOMETHING"], index 2 = "SOMETHING". That's correct! So line 84 is correct. Only line 83 (with the extra NACH segment) needs index 3. 

Hmm, but actually wait. Is the NACH one really `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES`? The customer's raw text: `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES`. Split by dash: ["ACH", "BD", "NACH", "ACME INDUSTRIES"]. Yes index 3 = "ACME INDUSTRIES".

But hold on — is the NACH a separator? In Indian banking, NACH- debit references often look like `ACH-BD-NACH-<merchant>`. So the merchant name is after the 3rd dash. So line 83 should be sep_by_dash(desc, 3, 4).

But also there's the issue: what if the merchant name contains a dash? Then splitting would break. But the given example "ACME INDUSTRIES" has no dash. Let me just fix to (3,4). Actually, maybe better: since names may contain dashes, maybe use a regex. But the existing pattern in HDFC line 169 uses `sep_by_dash(transactions.description,3,4)` for `^ACH D- BD-NACH-`. So for IDBI's `^ACH-BD-NACH`, using (3,4) is consistent. Good.

Now, let me verify by setting up the environment and testing. Let me check setup.py and dependencies and try running.

from flask import Flask, g
import logging
import warnings
import pandas as pd

app = Flask(__name__)

# ignore all caught warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

app.logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

# to capture the start time of request
@app.before_request
def before_request():
    g.start = time.time()

# to calculate total time taken for request-respone
@app.after_request
def after_request(response):
    diff = time.time() - g.start
    app.logger.info(f"Total time taken : {diff}s")
    return response

pd.set_option('display.max_rows', None)

import categorizationapp.Controller

steps.reasoning_content:
HDFC line 100: `remark.len().eq(16) & CREDIT & ^[0]{11}[0-9]{5}` -> CHEQUE_DEPOSIT. So a cheque deposit credit where remark is 16 digits starting with eleven 0s then 5 digits. That's narrow. But for a DEBIT cheque paid, HDFC relies on description containing "CHQ" etc. If description is just the cheque number... hmm.

Actually wait, let me reconsider the cheque case. The customer says "I wrote a cheque against my account" (DEBIT) "the app just lists it as a general transfer". "The only thing sitting in that row's note is the cheque's own number, nothing else around it." So the note (description or remark) is just the cheque number.

This is likely a bank where cheque detection relies on `remark` being exactly a certain digit count, and the customer's cheque number has a different count.

Let me look at banks that have a cheque rule on remark digit count but maybe an off-by-one. Let me re-examine each:
- YES: `^[0-9]{3,6}$` -> range 3-6. That's broad, would catch most.
- UCO: `^[0-9]{3,6}$` broad.
- PNB: `^[0-9]{5,6}$` -> 5 or 6 digits.
- Kotak: `^[0-9]{3,5}$` -> 3-5.
- KarurVyasa: `^00[0-9]{4}$` -> 6 digits starting 00.
- Indusind: `^[0-9]{5}$` -> exactly 5.
- BankOfMaharashtra: `^[0-9]{5,6}$`.
- Axis: `^[0-9]{5,6}$`.
- BOB: `^[0-9]{3,5}$` -> 3-5.
- HDFC: 16 with prefix.
- TamilnadMerchantile: `^[0-9]{5,}/` (with slash).
- SouthIndian: `[0-9]{6}` (no anchor, 6 digits anywhere).

Hmm. So which one has a bug where a cheque number falls to TRANSFER? Indusind `^[0-9]{5}$` requires exactly 5. A 6-digit cheque would fall through. But "earlier cheques were tagged correctly" implies earlier ones were 5 digits and worked; now 6-digit doesn't. But that seems like a real limitation, not a "regression".

Actually wait — maybe the bug isn't in the bank script's category rule but in the index/extraction. Let me reconsider. Actually, maybe case 1 is also in IDBI? Let me re-look at IDBI cheque rules.

IDBI line 55-58:
- 55: `[A-Z]+` & no special char & no digit & DEBIT -> CHEQUE_PAID. (pure letters)
- 56: `[0-9]{6}-|^WTHDRL,CLG|CHQ Paid` & DEBIT -> CHEQUE_PAID
- 57: `[A-Z]+` ... & CREDIT -> CHEQUE_DEPOSIT
- 58: `[0-9]{6}-|^BY INST|CHQ\s?DEP` & CREDIT -> CHEQUE_DEPOSIT

And partyName line 109: `remark contains [0-9]{4,6}` & category in [CHEQUE_PAID, CHEQUE_DEPOSIT] -> description.

Hmm. For IDBI, a cheque paid (DEBIT) where the note is just a cheque number. The category rule 56 needs `[0-9]{6}-` (6 digits followed by dash) or "CHQ Paid". A pure 6-digit number without dash wouldn't match `[0-9]{6}-`. So it would fall to TRANSFER. But is this IDBI? The customer says "earlier cheques were tagged correctly" — earlier cheques had descriptions like "CHQ Paid" or "123456-...". 

Hmm, this is getting hard to pin down without more info. Let me step back and reconsider the whole incident. It mentions "among them a note or reference that is exactly a certain number of digits wide". This phrasing "a note or reference that is exactly a certain number of digits wide" matches the GBHDL case (the `^[A-Z]{5}[0-9]{10,}`) — but that's case 3. And the cheque case is "exactly the cheque's own number". 

Let me reconsider: maybe the cheque case is a bank where the rule uses `remark.str.contains("[0-9]{6}")` without anchors, like SouthIndian. Or maybe it's the SBI cheque. Let me look at SBI.

steps.reasoning_content:
SBI cheque detection (line 45): description contains CLG/BY CLEARING/TO CLEARING/CHQ/CHEQUE/CHEQ NO & DEBIT -> CHEQUE_PAID. So if the description is just a cheque number with no "CHQ"/"CLG" text, it falls to TRANSFER. But "the only thing sitting in that row's note is the cheque's own number" — so the description is the cheque number. Hmm. But SBI cheque transactions usually have "CHQ" in description. 

Actually wait — the "note" might be the `remark` field, while `description` has the cheque text. The customer pasted the "raw narration" / "note". Hmm, "the raw narration each customer pasted". So the raw narration IS the description (or remark). 

Let me reconsider. Actually maybe the cheque case is a bank where the cheque rule keys off the `remark` field being exactly N digits, and the bug is the digit count. Let me look at banks where remark cheque rule has an unusual fixed width that might be wrong.

Actually, let me reconsider the whole thing differently. The 5 cases may map to specific known-bad lines. Let me think about what "regression" means given there's only one commit. Actually the incident is framed as a regression ("My earlier cheques were tagged correctly", "it used to lift the employer's name", "is not being pulled through anymore"). So the codebase AS-IS contains the regressed (buggy) code. My job is to fix the buggy lines to restore correct behavior.

Given cases 2 and 3 were clearly IDBI off-by-one index/dash bugs, the other cases are probably similar small bugs. Let me search for cases 4 and 5.

Case 4: mandate token charge -> should be ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION but filed as TRANSFER. "a small nominal amount, with the note flagging it as a mandate". So a description containing "mandate" with a small amount, should be ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION. Let me search for ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION rules and mandate.

Case 4: mandate token charge should be ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION but filed as TRANSFER. Let me look at Axis (lines 24-28) and Indusind (29-31). Both have a rule: amount==1 & description contains "MANDATE" -> ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION. Wait, the token charge is "a small nominal amount" — likely Rs 1 or Rs 2. "with the note flagging it as a mandate".

Indusind line 30: `amount.eq(2) & description contains MANDATE -> ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION`. Axis line 25: `amount.eq(1) & description contains MANDATE -> ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION`.

Hmm. Let me think. The token charge for mandate setup. Some banks post Rs 1, some Rs 299, etc. The bug: a bank that should map mandate-token to ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION but a condition with wrong amount or description makes it fall to TRANSFER.

Actually, wait — re-reading case 4: "The token confirmation charge a bank posts when a new auto-debit mandate is set up - a small nominal amount, with the note flagging it as a mandate - is being filed as an ordinary transfer instead of being recognized as a verification entry."

So it should be a "verification entry" = ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION. The note flags it as a mandate. Small nominal amount. 

Look at Axis line 25: `amount.eq(1) & contains MANDATE -> ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION`. If the token amount is Rs 1 this works. But what if it's some other small amount? Hmm.

Actually let me reconsider. Maybe the bug is the ordering: a mandate-token with amount 1 might be caught by a TRANSFER/default or by AUTO_MANDATE_CHARGES instead. Wait the ticket says it's filed as "ordinary transfer" not as mandate charges. So it's not even hitting AUTO_MANDATE_CHARGES.

Let me look at Axis lines 24-28 more fully and the surrounding.

card_purpose doesn't re-categorize CARD_PAYMENT_REVERSAL for POS credits on CREDIT_CARD. So for a CREDIT_CARD Indusind account, a POS TXN credit would be categorized by the Indusind bank script. With the typo fix (line 26 requires SAVING), a CREDIT_CARD account POS TXN credit → neither 26 nor 27 → falls to... let me check what catches POS TXN CREDIT on Indusind. Actually line 61 (CARD_PAYMENT) is DEBIT-only. So POS TXN CREDIT on Indusind → default TRANSFER. Then card_clean_names runs. Let me check card_clean_names — does it handle reversals? 

Hmm. This is getting deep, and I'm spending a lot. The key insight: the Indusind "SAVINGS" typo is clearly a bug (only occurrence in repo, and the canonical account type is "SAVING"). Fixing it restores the CARD_PAYMENT_REVERSAL branch for SAVING accounts. Whether the "different account type that's correct" is CREDIT_CARD or not, the fix is correct: it makes the affected (SAVING) POS reversals labeled as CARD_PAYMENT_REVERSAL instead of REFUND, which is exactly what the ticket demands ("should read as a card reversal"). And it doesn't disturb other transactions (CURRENT unchanged, <10 unchanged, non-POS unchanged).

I'm confident in all 5 fixes. Let me also reconsider: did the ticket say the affected see "plain refund" — and after my fix SAVING≥10 → reversal, SAVING<10 → refund. So some SAVING POS credits (small, <10) still → refund. Is that OK? The ticket's affected customers have a "refund that came back on a card" — a real card reversal, which is typically ≥10 (the purchase amount). So those become reversal. Small Re 1 POS things (rare) stay refund. Acceptable and surgical.

Now let me reconsider whether I should verify the FULL pipeline (Controller) for one case end-to-end, e.g., the IDBI salary NACH through salary_detection, to make sure nothing else breaks. But salary_detection needs multiple transactions and is complex. The partyName fix is what matters for case 2 (displayed name). Let me just confirm the IDBI categorize gives partyName "Acme Industries" (done). Good enough.

steps.message:
All five reported forms fixed. Each is a surgical, deterministic-labeling correction verified to reproduce-and-resolve and to leave currently-correct rows untouched:

| # | Form (verbatim) | Bank / file | Root cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cheque note = cheque number only → filed as TRANSFER | `Indusind.py:64` | `remark` cheque rule was exact-width `^[0-9]{5}$`; 6-digit cheque numbers fell through to TRANSFER (peers use `{5,6}`) | `^[0-9]{5,6}$` — 6-digit cheques now `CHEQUE_PAID`; 5-digit & 7-digit+ unchanged |
| 2 | `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES` → name shows `NACH` | `IDBI.py:83` | `sep_by_dash(desc,2,3)` grabbed segment index 2 (`NACH`) instead of the employer segment | `sep_by_dash(desc,3,4)` → `ACME INDUSTRIES`; plain `^ACH-BD-` (line 84) untouched |
| 3 | `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR` → name blank | `IDBI.py:110` | `py_extract(..., "[0-9]{10,} (.*)", index=1)` — pattern has one capture group, so index 1 returns nothing | `index=0` → `RAVI KUMAR` |
| 4 | Mandate-setup token (small amt, note flags mandate) → TRANSFER, should be verification | `BankOfMaharashtra.py:36-37` | `amount.isin(["1","-1"])` / `isin(["1"])` compared numeric amounts to **string** `"1"` → never matched, so verification rules were dead | `isin([1,-1])` / `isin([1])` — Rs-1 mandate/verification tokens now `ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION` |
| 5 | Card refund → plain `REFUND`, should be card reversal; varies by account category | `Indusind.py:26` | `accountType=="SAVINGS"` — the canonical value is `"SAVING"` (the only `"SAVINGS"` in the repo), so the `CARD_PAYMENT_REVERSAL` branch never fired and SAVING POS credits fell to the next rule (`REFUND`) | `accountType=="SAVING"` — SAVING POS-credit reversals (≥10) now `CARD_PAYMENT_REVERSAL`; small-amount (<10) and CURRENT rows unchanged |

Verification (representative):
- IDBI: `ACH-BD-NACH-ACME INDUSTRIES`→`Acme Industries`; `GBHDL0123456789 RAVI KUMAR`→`Ravi Kumar`; `ACH-BD-BENEFICIARY CO` still `Beneficiary Co`.
- Indusind: remark `12345`/`123456`→`CHEQUE_PAID`, `1234567`→`TRANSFER`; SAVING `POS TXN …` 1500→`CARD_PAYMENT_REVERSAL`, 5→`REFUND`, CURRENT→`TRANSFER`.
- BankOfMaharashtra: `MANDATE VERIFICATION` (₹1)→`ACCOUNT_VERIFICATION`; `TRANSFER TO XYZ` (₹500)→`TRANSFER`.

No tests exist in the repo; all modules import and compile cleanly. I did not commit, per the incident/release-blocker context — review the diff above before shipping.
